knowledge: Basic understanding of functions and modules in Python

sections.title: Overview
sections.content_md:
Layered architecture is a design pattern that organizes code into horizontal layers, each with a distinct responsibility. Each layer only talks to the layer directly below it, creating a clear chain of delegation. When you modify one layer (e.g., switching databases), the layers above it remain unaffected -- as long as the interface between them stays the same.

Think of it like a well-organized kitchen: the front-of-house (waiter) takes the order, passes it to the kitchen (chef), who fetches ingredients from the pantry (storage). The waiter never goes into the pantry, and the pantry does not know what dish is being made.

In WellBegun, this pattern appears on both the backend (Python/FastAPI) and the frontend (Svelte/TypeScript), and understanding it will help you know exactly where to put new code.

key_takeaways: Code is organized into layers: presentation (routers), business logic (services), data access (models/ORM)
Each layer only depends on the layer directly below it
Changes in one layer do not ripple through the others (as long as interfaces are preserved)
Both the backend and frontend follow this pattern independently

sections.title: Core Concepts
sections.content_md:
### The Three Backend Layers

```
HTTP Request
    |
    v
[Presentation Layer]  --  Routers (src/wellbegun/routers/)
    |                     Handles HTTP: parsing requests, returning responses,
    |                     status codes, error translation
    v
[Business Logic Layer]  --  Services (src/wellbegun/services/)
    |                       Contains the rules: what happens when you create,
    |                       update, delete. Orchestrates operations.
    v
[Data Access Layer]  --  Models + Database (src/wellbegun/models/, database.py)
                         Knows how to store and retrieve data.
```

### Layer Responsibilities

**Presentation Layer (Routers)**: Receives HTTP requests, validates input via Pydantic schemas, calls services, translates errors to HTTP status codes, shapes responses.

**Business Logic Layer (Services)**: Contains actual logic (creating entities, managing tags, cleanup). Does NOT know about HTTP. Receives plain Python arguments, returns Python objects.

**Data Access Layer (Models + ORM)**: Defines database schema as Python classes. Provides sessions for transactions. Translates Python operations to SQL.

### Dependency Direction

Upper layers depend on lower layers, never the reverse:
- Routers import services
- Services import models
- Models do NOT import services or routers

### The Frontend Layers

```
User Interaction
    |
    v
[Components]  --  Svelte components
    v
[Stores]  --  State management
    v
[API Client]  --  HTTP communication
```

Components call stores. Stores call API functions. API functions call the backend.

key_takeaways: Presentation layer handles HTTP concerns: request parsing, response shaping, status codes
Business logic layer contains rules and orchestration, independent of HTTP
Data access layer defines schema and manages persistence
Dependencies flow downward: routers -> services -> models, never upward
Frontend mirrors the pattern: components -> stores -> API client

sections.title: In This Project

code_examples.annotation: Presentation layer: receives HTTP POST, validates via NoteCreate schema, delegates to service, returns 201.

code_examples.code:
def create(db: Session, title: str, content: str | None = None) -> Note:
    note = Note(title=title, content=content)
    db.add(note)
    db.flush()
    create_entity_tag(db, title, "note", "note", note.id)
    db.commit()
    db.refresh(note)
    return note
code_examples.annotation: Business logic layer: creates Note, manages entity tags, commits transaction. No HTTP concepts.

code_examples.code:
class Note(Base):
    __tablename__ = "notes"
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    title: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(255), nullable=False)
    content: Mapped[str | None] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=True)
code_examples.annotation: Data access layer: defines the database schema. Does not know how it is used.
Frontend store layer: manages state, calls API client, notifies subscribers.
Frontend API client layer: handles HTTP communication with the backend.

sections.title: Guided Examples
sections.content_md:
### Tracing a Create-Note Request Through All Layers

**Step 1 -- Frontend Component**: User clicks 'Create':
```typescript
const newNote = await createNote({ title: "My Idea", content: "Some thoughts" });
```

**Step 2 -- Frontend API Client**: Sends HTTP POST:
```typescript
const res = await fetch('/api/notes/', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify({ title: "My Idea", content: "Some thoughts" })
});
```

**Step 3 -- Backend Router**: Validates body as NoteCreate, calls service:
```python
note_service.create(db, title=data.title, content=data.content)
```

**Step 4 -- Backend Service**: Creates Note, manages tags, commits:
```python
note = Note(title="My Idea", content="Some thoughts")
db.add(note)
db.flush()  # INSERT → id = 42
create_entity_tag(db, "My Idea", "note", "note", 42)
db.commit()
```

**Step 5 -- Backend Router**: Serializes via NoteOut, returns 201 with JSON.

**Step 6 -- Frontend API Client**: Parses JSON response.

**Step 7 -- Frontend Component**: Receives typed Note object, updates UI.

Data transforms at each boundary: JSON ↔ Pydantic schema ↔ plain arguments ↔ ORM objects ↔ SQL.

code_examples.annotation: Frontend API layer: constructs the HTTP request with JSON body and parses the response.
Backend router: extracts validated fields from schema and passes plain arguments to the service.

code_examples.code:
def create(db: Session, title: str, content: str | None = None) -> Note:
    note = Note(title=title, content=content)
    db.add(note)
    db.flush()
    create_entity_tag(db, title, "note", "note", note.id)
    db.commit()
    db.refresh(note)
    return note
code_examples.annotation: Backend service: orchestrates creation and tag management in a single transaction.

sections.title: Exercises
sections.content_md:
### Exercise 1: Identify the Layer

**Task**: For each code snippet, identify which layer it belongs to:
1. `raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Note not found")`
2. `db.query(Note).filter(Note.id == note_id).first()`
3. `__tablename__ = "notes"`
4. `note.is_active = True; db.commit()`
5. `response_model=list[NoteOut]`

<details>
<summary>Hint</summary>
1) Presentation -- HTTPException is HTTP-specific. 2) Service -- query within business logic. 3) Data access/model -- schema definition. 4) Service -- state modification and commit. 5) Presentation -- response shaping.
</details>

### Exercise 2: Where Does New Logic Go?

**Task**: For each requirement, identify which layer should change:
1. "When deleting a note, also delete all related knowledge triples"
2. "Return 403 Forbidden when a user tries to delete someone else's note"
3. "Add a word_count column to the notes table"
4. "When creating a note, auto-generate a summary using an LLM"

<details>
<summary>Hint</summary>
1) Service layer -- orchestrating related deletions is business logic. 2) Presentation layer -- HTTP status code translation. 3) Data access/model layer -- schema change. 4) Service layer -- LLM integration is business logic.
</details>

exercises.task: For five code snippets (HTTPException, db.query, __tablename__, setattr+commit, response_model), identify which architectural layer each belongs to.
hints: HTTPException and response_model are HTTP/presentation concerns.
db.query() and attribute modification + commit are service/business-logic operations.

exercises.task: For four new requirements (cascade delete, authorization error, new column, LLM summary), identify which layer should change and why.
hints: Cascade deletion is business logic (service layer).
HTTP status codes are presentation concerns (router layer).
Schema changes belong in the model layer.
External service integration (LLM) is business logic (service layer).

sections.title: Knowledge Check
sections.content_md: Test your understanding of layered architecture as applied in WellBegun.

questions.question: Which layer is responsible for returning HTTP 404 when a note is not found?
questions.explanation: The service returns None when not found. The router translates this into HTTPException(status_code=404). The service should not know about HTTP status codes.

questions.question: Why does the service layer accept plain Python arguments (title: str) rather than Pydantic schemas (NoteCreate)?
options: Pydantic is too slow for the service layer
So the service can be called from any context (API, CLI, tests) without HTTP-specific types
questions.explanation: If the service accepted NoteCreate, it would be coupled to the API layer. Plain arguments allow the same function to be called from CLI tools, tests, or background jobs.

questions.question: In the frontend, why does the store call the API client instead of using fetch directly?
options: Separation of concerns: the store manages state, the API client handles HTTP
The API client is faster than fetch
questions.explanation: The store manages state and notifies subscribers. The API client constructs requests and parses responses. If the API URL changes, only the API client needs updating.

questions.question: Which direction do dependencies flow in a layered architecture?
options: Lower layers depend on upper layers
Upper layers depend on lower layers
All layers depend on each other
questions.explanation: Routers import services, services import models. Models never import services or routers. This one-way chain keeps layers decoupled and independently replaceable.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.content_md: Curated resources for deepening your understanding of layered architecture.
